label: 1719 CE
era_summary: Great Northern War in its final months (Charles XII killed Dec 1718, Treaty of Nystad two years away) while the War of the Quadruple Alliance pits Spain against Britain, France, Austria and the Dutch. The Ottoman Tulip Period brings cultural flowering under Ahmed III; the Safavids are collapsing as Mahmud Hotaki's Afghans push toward Isfahan. In India the Mughals limp under Muhammad Shah and the Sayyid Brothers while the Marathas under Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath consolidate the Deccan. Qing China prospers in Kangxi's final years, Tokugawa Japan is closed and stable, and Yarubid Oman is pushing the Portuguese off the Swahili coast.

rome_italy.summary: Fragmented patchwork: Papal States, Venice (post-Karlowitz peace), Savoy, Spanish-then-Austrian Naples. Treaty of London 1718 has just transferred Sicily/Sardinia between the powers; no unified leadership. Religion: Roman Inquisition still active in many states; Jewish ghettos enforced in Rome, Venice, Ferrara, Mantua.
rome_italy.ruler: Pope Clement XI; multiple secular rulers (Savoy, Venice, Habsburg, etc.)

andalusia_iberia.summary: Spain under Philip V, embroiled in the War of the Quadruple Alliance (1718-20) versus Britain/France/Austria/Netherlands after defeat at Cape Passaro 1718. Cardinal Alberoni's adventurism collapsing; Bourbon reforms beginning. Portugal stable under João V's gold-fed reign. Active conflicts: Kakonda-Portugal (Angola), 1716-22; Spain-Austria, Britain, France, United Provinces, 1717-20 Religion: Spanish + Portuguese Inquisitions active; Moriscos long-expelled; New Christians (conversos) prosecuted. Catholicism compulsory.
andalusia_iberia.ruler: Philip V (Spain, Bourbon); João V (Portugal, Braganza)

francia.summary: France under Louis XV (age 9) with Philippe II d'Orléans as regent. Domestically stable but financially strained; John Law's Mississippi scheme inflating. Allied with Britain in the Quadruple Alliance; French troops cross into Spain in 1719. Dutch Republic stable under Stadtholderless rule. Active conflicts: Algiers-United Provinces, 1715-26; Spain-Austria, Britain, France, United Provinces, 1717-20 Religion: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685) still in force — Huguenots persecuted, exiled or underground (Camisard war ended 1710). Catholicism mandatory. Dutch Republic notably tolerant.
francia.ruler: Louis XV under Régence of Philippe II d'Orléans

england.summary: Great Britain under George I (Hanover), post-Jacobite-1715 and post-1719 Glenshiel Spanish landing failure. Whig supremacy; Robert Walpole's ascent imminent. Allied with France in the Quadruple Alliance; sea power dominant. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Britain-Sweden, 1715-19; Spain-Austria, Britain, France, United Provinces, 1717-20 Religion: Toleration Act 1689 — Protestant Dissenters tolerated; Catholics barred from public life by Test/Penal Laws. Jews resettled and de-facto tolerated in London.

rus.summary: Peter the Great in the closing phase of the Great Northern War; St Petersburg the new capital since 1712; sweeping centralizing reforms (Senate 1711, Colleges 1717-21, Table of Ranks 1722 incoming). Heavy conscription and taxation; tsarevich Alexei executed 1718. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Prussia-Sweden, 1715-20 Religion: Russian Orthodox state; Holy Synod replacing the Patriarchate 1721. Old Believers double-taxed since 1716. Western experts tolerated; Jews barred from interior.

constantinople_balkans.summary: Ottoman Tulip Period (Lale Devri) under Ahmed III and Grand Vizier Damat Ibrahim Pasha after the costly Treaty of Passarowitz 1718. Peace, court culture, the first Ottoman Turkish printing press (Müteferrika, 1727). Loss of Banat/Belgrade to Austria absorbed. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21 Religion: Millet system: Greek Orthodox, Armenian Apostolic, Jewish communities self-govern under their own law; non-Muslims pay cizye but practice openly. Among the most tolerant European-edge regimes for Jews.
constantinople_balkans.ruler: Sultan Ahmed III; Grand Vizier Damat Ibrahim Pasha

persia.summary: Safavid Empire in terminal collapse. Sultan Husayn weak and pious; Mahmud Hotaki's Ghilzai Afghans, having sacked Kerman 1719, are pushing toward Isfahan (sacked 1722). Russian Caspian campaign and Ottoman incursions follow. Active conflicts: Afghanistan-Persia, 1717-19 Religion: Twelver Shia state, hostile to Sunnis (which fuels the Afghan revolt), Christians and Zoroastrians. Armenian merchants of New Julfa partially protected.

central_asia.summary: Three Uzbek khanates (Bukhara under the late Ashtarkhanids, Khiva, Kokand emerging) — small, factional, frequently raided. The Dzungar Khanate to the east is the regional military power, fighting both Qing China (Tibet expedition 1718-20) and the Kazakhs. Active conflicts: China-Tibet, 1718-20 Religion: Sunni Hanafi Islam dominant with strong Naqshbandi Sufi influence. Small dhimmi Jewish (Bukharian) communities. Dzungars are Tibetan Buddhist.
central_asia.ruler: Abu'l-Fayz Khan (Bukhara, Ashtarkhanid); Shir Ghazi Khan (Khiva); Tsewang Rabtan (Dzungar)

egypt_nile.summary: Ottoman eyalet of Egypt with Mamluk beys increasingly autonomous; provincial Levant (Damascus, Aleppo, Sidon, Jerusalem) under Ottoman governors. Periodic Mamluk faction fights but no major revolt in 1719. Religion: Ottoman millet system: Copts, Greek Orthodox, Maronites, Druze, Jews self-govern as dhimmis under jizya. Holy sites in Jerusalem disputed.
egypt_nile.ruler: Ottoman pasha + Mamluk beys (de facto); Sultan Ahmed III above

arabia.summary: Hejaz nominally Ottoman under the Sharif of Mecca; Najd tribal/fragmented (pre-Wahhabi, founded 1744). Oman under Yarubid Sultan bin Saif II expanding aggressively into the Swahili coast, capturing Portuguese forts and starting the 1719-47 wars with Persia and Portugal. Yemen Zaidi imamate of al-Mutawakkil Qasim. Active conflicts: Oman, 1719-47 Religion: Sunni dominant (Shafi'i/Hanbali); Zaidi Shia in Yemen; Ibadi Islam state-sponsored in Oman. Non-Muslims barred from Mecca/Medina; dhimmis tolerated elsewhere.
arabia.ruler: Sultan bin Saif II (Oman, Yarubid); Sharif Abdullah ibn Sa'id (Mecca); Imam al-Mutawakkil Qasim (Yemen)

ethiopia.summary: Gondarine period under Emperor Dawit III; court intrigue intense (he will be poisoned 1721). Regional rases gaining at the expense of imperial authority — onset of the Zemene Mesafint trajectory. Religion: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo state church; Muslims and traditional religions tolerated regionally; Beta Israel/Falasha present but pressured; European Catholic missions excluded since the 1632 expulsion.

han_china.summary: Kangxi Emperor's final years (dies 1722). Stable centralized rule, mature bureaucracy, prosperous interior. Qing-Dzungar war over Tibet active — Qing expedition retakes Lhasa 1720 after the 1717-18 Dzungar occupation. Active conflicts: China-Tibet, 1718-20 Religion: Confucian state ideology; Buddhism + Daoism tolerated; Tibetan Buddhism patronized; Chinese Rites Controversy souring relations with Catholic missions, but Jesuits still at court. Islam tolerated.

japan.summary: Tokugawa shogunate under Yoshimune (8th shogun, acc. 1716); Kyōhō Reforms underway — frugality edicts, land-survey, ageyumai rice tax. Pax Tokugawa; Edo population near 1 million. Korea under Joseon king Sukjong (final year, d. 1720), stable Neo-Confucian state. Religion: Sakoku in full force; Christianity banned with executions ongoing; fumie tests annual. Buddhism + Shinto state-aligned. Korea aggressively Neo-Confucian, persecuting Catholicism and shamanism.
japan.ruler: Tokugawa Yoshimune (Shogun); Emperor Nakamikado (figurehead); King Sukjong of Joseon (Korea)

north_america_east.summary: Thirteen-Colonies precursor: Massachusetts Bay, New York, Pennsylvania, Virginia, Carolinas, plus Georgia not yet founded. Stable but small populations (~470k). Iroquois Confederacy and Cherokee still autonomous and powerful. New France (Canada/Louisiana) under Governor-General Vaudreuil; Louisiana being colonized via John Law's Company of the West. Religion: Varies by colony: Congregationalist establishment in MA, Quaker tolerance in PA, Anglican establishment in VA + SC, Catholic gentry in MD. New France strictly Catholic. Native religions intact in the interior.
north_america_east.ruler: British royal/proprietary governors; Philippe de Rigaud de Vaudreuil (New France)

andes.summary: Viceroyalty of Peru under Viceroy Diego Morcillo Rubio de Auñón (Archbishop of Lima, serving as interim viceroy 1716-20). Potosí silver still flowing though declining; mita labor brutal. Catastrophic typhus/plague epidemic across Andes 1718-20. Stable colonial rule. Religion: Catholic compulsory; Peruvian Inquisition in Lima; ongoing 'extirpation of idolatries' campaigns in Andean highlands. Syncretism widespread.
andes.ruler: Diego Morcillo Rubio de Auñón (Viceroy of Peru)

brazil_amazon.summary: Portuguese colonial Brazil under the Council of Overseas (Conselho Ultramarino) in Lisbon and a Viceroy at Salvador. Minas Gerais gold rush in full swing (peak 1700-1750) — funding João V's spending and triggering interior settlement. Heavy enslaved-African economy; Amazon basin largely under Jesuit-led missions. Religion: Catholic compulsory; Portuguese Inquisition (Lisbon-based) prosecutes New Christians (conversos); Jesuit aldeamentos consolidate Indigenous peoples. African religions practiced covertly.
brazil_amazon.ruler: João V of Portugal, via Viceroy of Brazil (Vasco Fernandes César de Meneses from 1720)

sweden_empire.summary: Charles XII killed at Fredriksten Dec 1718; sister Ulrika Eleonora crowned 1719 after accepting parliamentary constraints — beginning of the 'Age of Liberty'. Sweden exhausted, finances ruined, Russia and Hanover invading Bohuslän and Pomerania. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Sweden-Saxony, Poland, 1709-19; Sweden-Denmark, 1709-20 Religion: Lutheran state church under the 1686 Church Law; Catholics and Jews effectively excluded; conversion away from Lutheranism criminalized.

denmark_norway.summary: Denmark-Norway personal union under absolutist Frederick IV, on the winning side of the Great Northern War. Naval gains in the Baltic; Tordenskjold's raids on Swedish coast. Economy taxed but state functional. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Sweden-Denmark, 1709-20 Religion: Lutheran state church (Danish Law of Christian V); Catholicism and Judaism restricted; pietist revival gaining at court.

swedish_baltic.summary: Swedish Estonia, Livonia and Ingria de-facto under Russian military occupation since 1710; Swedish administration in name only pending the Treaty of Nystad 1721. Cities (Reval, Riga, Narva) depopulated by 1710 plague and Russian campaigns. Religion: Lutheran majority (German Baltic nobility, urban burghers); Orthodox minority growing under Russian occupation; Jewish settlement legally restricted.
swedish_baltic.ruler: Nominal Swedish governors; de-facto Russian commanders under Peter I

habsburg_austria.summary: Charles VI's Habsburg Monarchy at its early-18th-century peak after Passarowitz 1718 (gains in Banat, Belgrade, Oltenia). Rules Austria, Bohemia, Hungary and the new Austrian Netherlands; in the Quadruple Alliance against Spain. Pragmatic Sanction (1713) being pushed for inheritance. Active conflicts: Spain-Austria, Britain, France, United Provinces, 1717-20 Religion: Counter-Reformation Catholicism enforced in Bohemia and Inner Austria; Protestants tolerated in Hungary under the 1681 Sopron compromise; Jews restricted (small Vienna community).

prussia_brandenburg.summary: Brandenburg-Prussia under Frederick William I, the 'Soldier-King'. Rigorous fiscal-military reforms, doubled army, austerity at court. Just exiting the Great Northern War with gains in Swedish Pomerania (Stettin) confirmed in 1720. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Prussia-Sweden, 1715-20 Religion: Calvinist Hohenzollern ruling Lutheran majority; famously tolerant — refuge for Huguenots (1685 Edict of Potsdam) and Salzburg Protestants later; Jews tolerated under restrictions.

german_princes.summary: Patchwork of HRE states. Saxony (Augustus the Strong, also king of Poland) exhausted by Great Northern War; Hanover ruled in personal union from London by George I; Bavaria under Max II Emanuel rebuilding after Spanish-Succession defeats; ecclesiastical electorates and dozens of small territories stable under the post-Westphalia order. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Sweden-Saxony, Poland, 1709-19; Hanover-Sweden, 1719-21 Religion: Westphalia (1648) confessional settlement: prince chooses Catholic/Lutheran/Reformed; minorities tolerated where economically useful. Saxony's electors Catholic but state Lutheran.
german_princes.ruler: Augustus II 'the Strong' (Saxony); George I (Hanover, in personal union with GB); Max II Emanuel (Bavaria); various ecclesiastical + secular princes

polish_lithuanian_commonwealth.summary: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth under Augustus II the Strong, badly weakened by the Great Northern War and Russian/Saxon armies marching through. The Silent Sejm of 1717 has just locked in noble dominance and capped the army at ~24,000 — start of the slow slide to partitions. Active conflicts: Denmark, Saxony, Poland, Russia, Prussia, Hanover, Britain-Sweden, Turkey, 1700-21; Sweden-Saxony, Poland, 1709-19 Religion: Historically Europe's most tolerant state (Warsaw Confederation 1573) but Catholicism increasingly aggressive — anti-Protestant Toruń tumult 1724 looming. Large Jewish population under the Council of Four Lands. Eastern Orthodox + Uniate Christians.

mughal_north.summary: Mughal Empire under teenage Muhammad Shah (acc. Sept 1719 after the Sayyid Brothers deposed Farrukhsiyar in Feb and ran through Rafi ud-Darajat and Shah Jahan II). Court dominated by the Sayyid Brothers (Hasan Ali, Husain Ali). Imperial authority crumbling in the provinces. Religion: Jizya on non-Muslims abolished 1712-19; Hindu nobility (Rajputs, Marathas in Mughal service) again influential; Sikh persecution easing post-Banda Singh execution 1716.
mughal_north.ruler: Muhammad Shah (Mughal); real power: Sayyid Brothers

maratha_confederacy.summary: Maratha Confederacy under Chhatrapati Shahu I with Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath, who in Feb-April 1719 negotiated the landmark agreement granting Marathas chauth + sardeshmukhi rights over the six Deccan subahs and home rule over Shahu's Swarajya. Marathas escort the Sayyid Brothers to Delhi during the 1719 coup. Religion: Hindu state with strong Brahmin Peshwa leadership; Muslims, Jains, Parsis tolerated; Sikh + bhakti movements active. Comparatively pluralist by 1719 South Asian standards.
maratha_confederacy.ruler: Chhatrapati Shahu I; Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath

bengal.summary: Bengal Subah de-facto independent under Nawab Murshid Quli Khan since 1717 (officially nazim + diwan). Capital moved to Murshidabad; revenue system overhauled, jagirdars curbed. Wealthiest Mughal province; English, Dutch, French factories at Calcutta/Chinsurah/Chandernagore. Religion: Muslim ruling class over Hindu majority; broadly tolerant administration. Sufi influence strong; Hindu zamindars retained.
bengal.ruler: Murshid Quli Khan, Nawab of Bengal (nominally under Mughal Muhammad Shah)

deccan_sultanates.summary: Mughal Deccan in flux: Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I will formally found the autonomous Hyderabad State in 1724 but is already a power broker in 1719. Mysore under Krishnaraja Wodeyar II nominally rules, Dalwai Devarajaiah in real control. Maratha chauth pressure constant. Religion: Sunni Muslim ruling elites over Hindu majority; sharia-based courts coexist with local custom; Marathas extract chauth from non-Muslim and Muslim subjects alike.
deccan_sultanates.ruler: Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I (Mughal Deccan subadar); Krishnaraja Wodeyar II / Dalwai Devarajaiah (Mysore)

voc_indonesia.summary: Dutch East India Company (VOC) the dominant power in the Indonesian archipelago, capital at Batavia. Governor-General Hendrick Zwaardecroon (1718-25) consolidating spice monopoly; Mataram Sultanate (Java) under Pakubuwono I a VOC client. Bantam, Aceh, Makassar reduced or subordinated. Religion: Native population overwhelmingly Sunni Muslim (Java, Sumatra), Hindu (Bali), animist. VOC enforces Dutch Reformed in fort chaplaincies; Catholics barred; broad de-facto tolerance of Islam for trade reasons.
voc_indonesia.ruler: Hendrick Zwaardecroon (VOC Governor-General); Pakubuwono I (Mataram, dies Feb 1719)

siam_vietnam_burma.summary: Ayutthaya Kingdom under King Thai Sa (Phumintharacha) stable, prosperous, post-1688 expulsion of French; foreign trade flourishing. Vietnam split: Trịnh lords (Trịnh Cương) at Thăng Long (north), Nguyễn lords (Nguyễn Phúc Chu) at Phú Xuân (south). Toungoo Burma under Taninganwe Min weakening — only ~33 years from Mon revolt + Konbaung rise. Religion: Theravada Buddhism dominant (Siam, Burma); Mahayana + Confucianism (Vietnam); Catholic and Muslim minorities tolerated as trading communities. Pluralism is the regional norm.
siam_vietnam_burma.ruler: Thai Sa / Phumintharacha (Ayutthaya); Trịnh Cương (Đàng Ngoài); Nguyễn Phúc Chu (Đàng Trong); Taninganwe Min (Toungoo Burma)

spanish_philippines.summary: Spanish colonial Philippines under Governor-General Fernando Bustamante (assassinated by a friar-led mob in Manila Oct 1719). Manila Galleon trade with Acapulco is the colony's economic spine; Moros (Sultanates of Sulu, Maguindanao) independent in the south. Religion: Roman Catholic, enforced by friars (Dominican, Franciscan, Augustinian, Jesuit); indigenous religions suppressed in lowland Christianized areas. Muslim south outside colonial control; Chinese Sangley population periodically persecuted.

sources.label: Fernando Manuel de Bustamante y Bustillo

spanish_philippines.ruler: Fernando Manuel de Bustamante y Bustillo (Governor-General); Philip V of Spain above

kongo_angola.summary: Kingdom of Kongo fragmented after the 1665 Battle of Mbwila and ongoing civil wars; rival claimants (Kimpanzu, Kinlaza) hold pieces. Portuguese Angola coast (Luanda, Benguela) is a slave-trade hub feeding Brazil. Queen Nzinga's Matamba/Ndongo legacy persists. Active conflicts: Kakonda-Portugal (Angola), 1716-22 Religion: Kongo officially Catholic since 1491, heavily syncretic; Antonine movement (Beatriz Kimpa Vita executed 1706) recently suppressed. Traditional religions dominant in interior; Catholic-only on Portuguese coast.
kongo_angola.ruler: Manuel II of Kongo (contested); Portuguese governors of Angola

southern_africa.summary: Lunda Empire under Mwata Yamvo and Luba Kingdom dominate the central African savanna; Rozvi Empire on the Zimbabwe plateau replacing the Mutapa. Portuguese Mozambique restricted to Sofala/Mozambique Island. Dutch Cape Colony small (founded 1652) — frontier settlement, slave imports, Khoekhoe smallpox of 1713 devastating. Religion: Traditional African religions dominant. Dutch Reformed at the Cape (no toleration for Catholics, partial for Muslims via Cape Malay exiles). Portuguese ports Catholic.
southern_africa.ruler: Mwata Yamvo Muteba (Lunda); various Luba, Rozvi rulers; Maurits Pasques de Chavonnes (VOC Cape governor)

asante_coast.summary: Asante Empire under Asantehene Opoku Ware I (acc. 1718, Osei Tutu died 1717) consolidating gold and slave-trade dominance inland from the Gold Coast. Coastal Dahomey under King Agaja expanding aggressively (Allada conquered 1724). European trading forts (Dutch, English, Danish, Brandenburg) dot the coast. Active conflicts: Pirates-England (Gambia), 1719 Religion: Akan, Fon and Yoruba traditional religions dominant; Islam present among northern Asante traders; Christianity confined to European fort chaplaincies — minimal native conversion.
asante_coast.ruler: Opoku Ware I (Asante); Agaja (Dahomey); Akwasi Akromampong (Akwamu)

sahel_interior.summary: Mali Empire reduced to a rump; Bambara Kingdom of Segou founded 1712 under Mamari 'Biton' Coulibaly rising on the Niger. Kanem-Bornu Empire under Mai Dunama VIII still significant around Lake Chad; Hausa city-states (Kano, Katsina, Zaria) prosperous trade hubs. Religion: Islam dominant among elites and traders (Maliki Sunni); traditional religions widespread among commoners and in Bambara Segou (notably non-Muslim). Tolerant coexistence the norm.
sahel_interior.ruler: Mamari Coulibaly (Segou Bambara); Mai Dunama VIII (Bornu); various Hausa kings

new_spain.summary: Viceroyalty of New Spain (Mexico + Central America + parts of US Southwest + Philippines administratively) under Viceroy Baltasar de Zúñiga, marqués de Valero. Stable bureaucracy, silver from Zacatecas/Guanajuato funding the empire; northern frontier (Texas missions re-established 1716-19) active. Religion: Catholicism compulsory; Mexican Inquisition active against Jews/conversos, witches, heretics, syncretism. Indigenous religions formally suppressed; popular syncretism (Guadalupe) tolerated.

sources.label: Baltasar de Zúñiga, marqués de Valero

new_spain.ruler: Baltasar de Zúñiga, marqués de Valero (Viceroy); Philip V of Spain above

caribbean.summary: Multi-imperial: Spanish Cuba/Puerto Rico/Santo Domingo, English Jamaica/Barbados/Leewards, French Saint-Domingue/Martinique/Guadeloupe, Dutch Curaçao/Aruba/Bonaire, Danish St Thomas. Golden Age of Piracy ending (Blackbeard killed 1718); War of the Quadruple Alliance brings Anglo-Spanish raiding. Sugar+slavery economy at takeoff. Active conflicts: Pirates-England (Gambia), 1719 Religion: Catholic compulsory in Spanish/French colonies; Anglican established in English; Dutch Reformed in Dutch; Jews tolerated in Curaçao (Mikvé Israel-Emanuel, 1659) and Barbados. Enslaved African religions suppressed but persistent.
caribbean.ruler: Various colonial governors under Spain, Britain, France, Dutch Republic, Denmark-Norway
